You said: lets do the supabase

lets do the supabase

9:44 PM

Claude responded: Good — let's get the backend live.

Good — let's get the backend live. I'll give you everything in order: the schema to run, then where to find the two values to paste back to me. You'll do three things on Supabase's side; I'll handle the code.

First, let me pull the current Supabase setup steps so the instructions match what their dashboard actually looks like right now.

Searched the web

That confirms an important decision for your situation. The built-in email service only sends 2 emails per day and is meant for basic testing, and with email confirmation enabled, signUp returns a user but no session until the user clicks the confirmation link. MailtrapRapid Developers

For a firefighter class signing up, that email-confirmation step would be a headache — students wouldn't get past signup without a working email link, and the free email service can't send enough of them. So I'll have you turn email confirmation OFF. That way students sign up with email + password and are immediately logged in, exactly like the local version works now. The tradeoff is that emails aren't verified as real, which is fine for a closed class where you know who's enrolling.

Here's your setup, in order.

Step 1 — Create the project

Go to supabase.com, sign in, and create a new project. You'll pick a name, a region (choose one near Florida, like East US), and a database password — save that somewhere; you won't need it for our app but you'll want it later. The project takes a couple of minutes to provision.

Step 2 — Turn off email confirmation

In your project: Authentication → Providers → Email, and toggle Confirm email OFF, then save. This makes signup-and-go work without email links.

Step 3 — Run the database schema

In your project: SQL Editor → New query, paste the entire script below, and click Run. It creates the three tables, the security rules, the leaderboard, and the auto-profile trigger.

Created a file, read a file

That SQL file is ready to paste into the SQL Editor. It builds everything: the three tables, security rules so students can only write their own data while you (and only your email) can manage questions, the class leaderboard, and the trigger that auto-creates a profile when someone signs up. I included the explicit API grants since new projects created after May 30, 2026 must add explicit Postgres grants for the auto-generated REST API. IT Path Solutions

Step 4 — Send me two values

Once the project exists, go to Project Settings → API (or Settings → API Keys). You need two things:

  1. Project URL — looks like https://abcdefgh.supabase.co
  2. anon public key — a long string labeled "anon" / "public". This one is safe to put in the HTML; it's designed to be public and the security rules above are what actually protect the data.

Paste both here and I'll wire them into the app, then it's live: shared accounts, shared question bank, real class leaderboard across all devices.

A couple of honest notes before you start:

Don't paste the service_role key. On the API page you'll see two keys — anon and service_role. The docs are explicit that the service_role key should never be exposed in the browser. Only send me the anon one. Supabase

The 7-day pause still applies. Since this is the free tier, if the project goes a week with zero activity it pauses until you unpause it from the dashboard. For a class using it regularly that's rarely an issue, and we can set up the auto-ping workaround later if you want.

Go ahead and do Steps 1–3, then send me the URL and anon key. If you hit anything confusing in the dashboard, tell me what you're seeing and I'll talk you through it.

Supabase setup
Code · SQL 

You said: where can i find the anon public key

where can i find the anon public key

9:59 PM

Claude responded: Let me give you the current location — Supabase moved the API keys page around recently, so I want to point you to the right spot.

Let me give you the current location — Supabase moved the API keys page around recently, so I want to point you to the right spot.

Searched the web

Important update from this: Supabase changed how keys work, and brand-new projects no longer have the old "anon" key at all. As of November 2025, new projects no longer get legacy keys. So depending on when you made your project, you'll grab one of two things — and both work identically in our app. Medium

Where to find it

In your project, go to Settings → API Keys. In most cases you can get keys from the project's Connect dialog, but if you want a specific key, you'll find them in the Settings → API Keys section of the dashboard. Supabase

Then look at which tab you have:

If you see a "Publishable key" (starts with sb_publishable_...) — that's the one. Copy it. This is the new format and it's what new projects get. The publishable key replaces the anon key and carries the same low privileges, so your Row Level Security policies behave the same. Supabase

If you see a "Legacy API Keys" tab with an "anon" key (a long string starting with eyJ...) — copy that one instead. For legacy keys, copy the anon key for client-side operations from the Legacy API Keys tab. Supabase

Either one is fine — they're interchangeable for what we're building, and the SQL security rules I gave you protect the data the same way regardless of which key format you have.

The one to avoid

Don't send the secret key (sb_secret_...) or the service_role key. Those bypass all the security rules and must never go in browser code. You want the publishable or anon one — the public-facing key.

So, send me two things:

  1. Project URL — from the same API settings page, looks like https://abcdefgh.supabase.co
  2. The publishable key OR the anon key — whichever your project has

Paste both and I'll wire them in. If you open the API Keys page and aren't sure which tab you're looking at, just tell me what tabs and key names you see and I'll point you to the right one.

You said: https://ghsnxxiekiiyentzlsxu.

https://ghsnxxiekiiyentzlsxu.supabase.co/rest/v1/

sb_publishable_y2QFsGwW06RdpnPJG1FQUw_ArqXWGUP

10:00 PM

Claude responded: Got both.

Got both. The publishable key is the new-format key, which works fine. Let me wire them in.

One small thing: the URL you pasted has /rest/v1/ on the end — that's the API endpoint path. The Supabase client needs just the base project URL, so I'll use https://ghsnxxiekiiyentzlsxu.supabase.co (without the trailing path).

Insert the Supabase project URL and publishable key into the app config

Now, one compatibility check. The search results noted that the new publishable keys behave slightly differently from the old anon keys in how they're sent in headers. The @supabase/supabase-js v2 client handles this automatically, but let me verify the app is loading a recent-enough version of the client library.

Check the Supabase JS library version and client creation

Good — it loads @supabase/supabase-js@2, which is the current major version and handles the new publishable key format. Let me do a final syntax check and confirm the backend is now active.

Ran a command, read a file

Done — the app is now connected to your Supabase backend. The badge in the top-right will read "shared" instead of "local" when you open it.

Before you rely on it, let's confirm the three pieces are actually working. Here's a quick test sequence:

1. Did the SQL run? If you haven't already pasted supabase-setup.sql into the SQL Editor and hit Run, do that first — without the tables, signup will fail. You'll know it worked if, in the dashboard under Table Editor, you see three tables: profiles, questions, and sessions.

2. Is email confirmation off? Check Authentication → Providers → Email and make sure Confirm email is toggled OFF. If it's on, signup will appear to hang because it's waiting for an email link.

3. Test it. Open the app and sign up with your own email (smorejon1@gmail.com). If everything's wired right:

  • You land straight in the app (no email confirmation step)
  • Your role shows "Admin · Instructor" in gold
  • You see the "Manage questions" tab
  • The topic cards show their question counts (182 built-in)
  • Take a short quiz, finish it, and check that it appears under "Recent sessions"

Then to confirm the shared part is real: open the app in a different browser (or have someone else open it), sign up as a different user, take a quiz, and confirm that user shows up on your leaderboard. That proves data is syncing through Supabase rather than sitting in one browser.

A couple of things to expect:

The built-in 182 questions live in the app file itself, so every student gets them automatically. When you add questions through the Manage questions tab now, those write to the shared database — so all students see them too, on any device. That's the upgrade you were after.

One realistic caveat: the very first signup also fires the profile-creation trigger from the SQL. If something in the schema didn't run cleanly, the symptom is usually "signup succeeds but the leaderboard or name looks wrong." If you see anything odd, tell me exactly what happens at which step and I'll diagnose it — Supabase also has a Logs section (under Authentication and under the database) that shows errors, which helps me pinpoint things.

Go ahead and run through the test. What happens when you sign up?

Trt study app backend
Code · HTML 
Error: Error: Failed to fetch
10:02 PM

Claude responded: "Failed to fetch" means the browser couldn't reach Supabase at all — the request didn't even complete.

"Failed to fetch" means the browser couldn't reach Supabase at all — the request didn't even complete. That's almost always one of a few specific things, and the good news is none of them are hard to fix. Let me narrow it down.

The most common cause here is how you're opening the file. If you double-clicked the HTML and the address bar starts with file://, browsers block it from making network calls to Supabase for security reasons. This is the #1 cause of "Failed to fetch."

A couple of quick questions to pin it down:

First — look at your browser's address bar when the app is open. Does it start with file:// (or just show a path like C:\Users\... or /Users/...)? Or does it start with http:// or https://?

If it's file://, that's the culprit. The app needs to be served over http, not opened as a local file. The fastest fixes:

  • Easiest: drag the file into a free static host. But since you'll want students to reach it anyway, this is the moment to put it online — GitHub Pages (which you've used before for the study hub) works perfectly and is free.
  • Quick local test: if you have Python, open a terminal in the file's folder and run python3 -m http.server, then visit http://localhost:8000/trt-study-app-backend.html. That serves it over http so the fetch works.

Second — it's worth confirming the SQL actually ran. If the tables don't exist yet, you'd get errors too (though usually a different message). In your Supabase dashboard under Table Editor, do you see profiles, questions, and sessions?

Third — there's a small chance it's the new publishable key needing a setting flipped. Let me check whether your project has the legacy keys enabled, since the brand-new key format occasionally needs the data API confirmed as on.

Tell me two things and I can zero in:

  1. What does the address bar show — file:// or http(s)://?
  2. Do the three tables show up in the Table Editor?

My strong guess is it's the file:// issue, since that produces exactly this error and you'd hit it the moment the app tries to talk to Supabase. If so, getting it onto GitHub Pages solves it and gets you ready for the class in one step — and I can walk you through that.

You said: how do i run the sql?

how do i run the sql? provide step by step guide i am new to claude and this coding type things

10:07 PM

Claude responded: No problem at all — you don't need any coding background for this.

No problem at all — you don't need any coding background for this. You'll be copying and pasting one block of text and clicking a button. Let me walk you through it slowly, click by click.

What you're about to do

Supabase has a built-in tool called the "SQL Editor." Think of it like a text box where you paste instructions, hit Run, and it sets up the storage your app needs (the tables that hold users, questions, and quiz scores). You only do this once.

Step-by-step

Step 1 — Open your Supabase project

Go to supabase.com and sign in. Click on your project (the one you created earlier) to open its dashboard.

Step 2 — Find the SQL Editor

Look at the menu running down the left side of the screen. You're looking for an icon/label that says SQL Editor. It often looks like a little database or terminal symbol. Click it.

Step 3 — Start a new query

Once the SQL Editor opens, look for a button that says "+ New query" (usually near the top-left or top of that panel). Click it. You'll get an empty white text box — that's where the instructions go.

Step 4 — Get the SQL text

Open the file I made you earlier called supabase-setup.sql. (It's one of the files I shared in this conversation — click to open it.) Select all of the text inside it and copy it. On a Mac that's Cmd+A then Cmd+C; on Windows it's Ctrl+A then Ctrl+C.

Step 5 — Paste it in

Click inside that empty white text box in the SQL Editor and paste (Cmd+V or Ctrl+V). The box should fill up with a couple hundred lines of text. That's expected — don't worry about understanding it.

Step 6 — Run it

Look for a green "Run" button (bottom-right of the editor, or top-right). Click it. There's also a keyboard shortcut — Cmd+Enter on Mac, Ctrl+Enter on Windows — but the button is easier.

Step 7 — Check it worked

After a second or two, a small panel at the bottom should say something like "Success. No rows returned." That green "Success" message is exactly what you want — it means everything got created. (If it shows a red error instead, copy the error text and paste it here — I'll tell you what to do.)

Step 8 — Confirm the tables exist

In that same left-side menu, click Table Editor. You should now see three tables listed: profiles, questions, and sessions. If they're there, the SQL worked.

A reassuring note: this step is safe. You can't break anything by running it, and if something doesn't look right you can just run it again — I wrote it so that re-running it causes no harm.

When you've done it, tell me what the bottom panel said (Success, or an error). And remember the "Failed to fetch" problem from before is likely a separate issue about how the app file is opened — so once the SQL is done, we'll tackle that next. One thing at a time.

What do you see after you hit Run?

supabase-setup.sql where is this file

10:10 PM

Claude responded: It's a file I created earlier in this conversation.

It's a file I created earlier in this conversation. It would have appeared as a small clickable attachment in my message — but scrolling back to find it is a hassle, so let me just give it to you fresh right here.

Presented file

There it is — supabase-setup should now show up as a clickable file right above this message.

You actually have two ways to get the text into Supabase, and the second might be easier for you:

Option A — open the file, copy from it
Click the supabase-setup file above to open it, select all the text (Cmd+A / Ctrl+A), copy it (Cmd+C / Ctrl+C), then paste into the Supabase SQL Editor.

Option B — copy straight from here
I can paste the entire SQL right into this chat as a text block, and you copy it directly from my message — no need to open a separate file at all. Some people find that simpler.

Want me to do Option B and drop the full text here so you can copy it straight across? If so, just say "paste it here" and I will. Otherwise, click the file above and follow the steps from before.

Either way, once it's pasted into the SQL Editor's white box, you hit the green Run button and watch for the "Success" message. Which option do you want?

10:12 PM

Claude responded: Excellent — that means the database is fully set up.

Excellent — that means the database is fully set up. Three tables created, security rules in place, leaderboard and signup trigger live. That was the hard part, and it's done.

Now back to the "Failed to fetch" error, because that's the thing still standing between you and a working app. As I mentioned, that error is almost always about how the app file is opened, not the database. So let's check that.

Look at your browser's address bar when the app is open. Does it start with:

  • file:// (or just show a path like C:\Users\... or /Users/yourname/...) — OR
  • http:// or https://

If it starts with file://, that's the whole problem. Browsers block files opened that way from talking to Supabase. The fix is to put the app online — which you need to do anyway so your students can reach it.

And here's the good news: you've done this before. Your original Study Hub is already live at a GitHub Pages address (smorejon1-a11y.github.io). We just put this new file in the same place.

So the next step is one of two things, depending on what you tell me:

If the address bar shows http:// or https:// and you still get the error — then it's something else (likely a key/setting issue), and I'll dig into that.

If it shows file:// — then we get the app onto GitHub Pages, same as your old one, and that fixes the error and makes it shareable with your class in one move.

My strong guess is it's file://, since that produces exactly this error. But tell me what the address bar shows first, so I don't send you down the wrong path.

What does it say?

You said: how do i open app i only see the code

how do i open app i only see the code

10:14 PM

Claude responded: Ah, that's the missing piece — you're looking at the code of the file, not the running app.

Ah, that's the missing piece — you're looking at the code of the file, not the running app. They're two different things. Right now you're seeing the recipe; you haven't cooked the meal yet.

Let me explain simply, then give you the fix.

An HTML file is like a document that a web browser turns into a working app. When you see all the code, you're looking at the raw file. To see the actual app — the login screen, the quiz, the buttons — a web browser (Chrome, Safari, etc.) has to open it.

But here's the catch we already talked about: even when you open it in a browser the normal way (by double-clicking), it'll show the app but give you that "Failed to fetch" error, because browsers block files opened directly from your computer from talking to Supabase.

So the real solution — the one that both shows the app AND fixes the error AND lets your students use it — is to put it online. And you've done this exact thing before with your original Study Hub.

Before I write you the step-by-step, let me ask one thing so I give you the right instructions, because there are a couple of ways to do this and I want to match what's easiest for you:

You said: Q: Your old Study Hub is already online at smorejon1-a11y.

Q: Your old Study Hub is already online at smorejon1-a11y.github.io. How do you usually put files there? A: I don't remember / not sure

10:14 PM

Claude responded: No worries — that's completely normal.

No worries — that's completely normal. Let me give you a clear, beginner-friendly path. We'll get the app online through the GitHub website, all point-and-click, no programs to install.

First, let me make sure I'm describing GitHub's current website layout accurately, since these sites change their buttons around.

Searched the web

First, let me get the app file ready for you to download, then I'll give you the full walkthrough.

Presented file

Here's the whole thing, broken into small steps. We're putting the app into the same GitHub place your old Study Hub lives, so it goes online and the "Failed to fetch" error disappears.

Part 1 — Download the app file to your computer

The file trt-study-app-backend is right above this message. Click it, and save/download it to somewhere easy to find, like your Downloads folder or Desktop. You should end up with a file named something like trt-study-app-backend.html on your computer.

Part 2 — Go to your GitHub project

  1. Open a new browser tab and go to github.com. Sign in if it asks.
  2. Your old Study Hub is at smorejon1-a11y.github.io/study-hub/, which means you have a GitHub project (a "repository") that holds it. After signing in, click your profile picture (top-right corner) and choose Your repositories.
  3. Click the repository in the list — it's likely named study-hub or smorejon1-a11y.github.io. (If you see more than one and aren't sure, tell me their names and I'll point to the right one.)

Part 3 — Upload the new file

Once you're inside the repository (you'll see a list of files):

  1. Look for a button near the top that says Add file — it's a dropdown. Click it. Above the list of files, select the Add file dropdown menu and click Upload files. GitHub
  2. Click Upload files from the little menu that drops down.
  3. On the next screen, drag your downloaded trt-study-app-backend.html file into the box — or click choose your files and pick it. Click the link that says "choose your files," which opens a file browser, and select the files you want to upload. GitHub
  4. Wait a moment for it to finish uploading (you'll see the filename appear).

Part 4 — Save it (this is called "committing")

  1. Scroll down. You'll see a box for a commit message — it doesn't matter what you type, something like "add study app" is fine. Add a commit message in the box under the list of files to be added. GitHub
  2. Click the green Commit changes button. Click the green button that says "Commit changes," and now your files will be uploaded to your repository. GitHub

Part 5 — Open the live app

Once it's committed, your app is online. The web address will follow the same pattern as your old one. If your repository is the study-hub one, the address will be:

https://smorejon1-a11y.github.io/study-hub/trt-study-app-backend.html

Type that into your browser. This time it'll show the actual app — login screen, not code — and because it's served over https://, the Supabase connection will work and the "Failed to fetch" error will be gone.

Take it one part at a time. The most likely spot to get stuck is Part 2 (finding the right repository), so if the repository list looks confusing, just tell me the names of what you see and I'll tell you exactly which one to click. Where are you once you start?

Trt study app backend
Code · HTML
